revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[de:Trockenlacktechnik]][[ja:乾漆造]][[ko:건칠조]][[zh:干漆夹苎]]
[[File:Todaiji Monaster Fukukensaku Kwannon of Hokkedo (232).jpg|thumb|200px|Fukūkensaku Kannon Standing Statue (Tōdai-ji, Hokkedō, {{ill|National Treasure (Japan)|en|National Treasure (Japan)|qid=Q1139795|lt=National Treasure of Japan|1=National Treasure (Japan)}})]] [[File:Eleven-faced Kannon Shorinji.JPG|thumb|200px|Eleven-faced Kannon Standing Statue (Shōrin-ji, Nara, National Treasure)]] [[File:NYOIRIN KANSHINJI.JPG|right|thumb|200px|Nyoirin Kannon Statue (Kanshin-ji, Osaka, National Treasure)]] '''Dry lacquer sculpture''' (乾漆造, Kanshitsuzō) is both a lacquerware technique and a method of producing sculptures in {{ill|East Asia|en|East Asia|qid=Q27231|lt=East Asia|1=East Asia}}. It involves layering hemp cloth or Japanese paper with lacquer<ref>This technique is comparable to modern {{ill|Fibre-reinforced plastic|en|Fibre-reinforced plastic|qid=Q263125|lt=fiber-reinforced plastic|1=Fibre-reinforced plastic}}.</ref>, or shaping a mixture of lacquer and wood powder to create forms. The origins of the technique are in {{ill|China|en|China|qid=Q148|lt=China|1=China}}, where it was called "jiaozhu" (夾紵) or "soku" (塞, 土偏に「塞」). It was used to create vessels, coffins, and sculptures. In {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}, it was widely used for producing Buddhist statues from the late 7th to the 8th century but declined in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} and beyond. == Types of Dry Lacquer Sculptures == Dry lacquer sculpture includes "Hollow Dry Lacquer" (脱活乾漆造, Dakkatsu Kanshitsuzō), formed by layering hemp cloth to a thickness of about one centimeter, and the simplified "Wood-core Dry Lacquer" (木心乾漆造, Mokushin Kanshitsuzō). === Hollow Dry Lacquer === The production method is as follows: First, a wooden core frame is constructed to form the skeletal structure of the statue, over which clay (sodo) is a11lied to shape its contours. Hemp cloth is layered over this framework using "mugi-urushi," a paste made from lacquer mixed with wheat flour (similar to modern flour paste). The cloth layers, typically about one centimeter thick, are built up to form the statue. Afterward, details are refined with "makkō urushi" (lacquer mixed with powdered cedar or pine leaves) or "kokusō urushi" (lacquer mixed with sawdust or textile waste). During the {{ill|Nara period|en|Nara period|qid=Q189178|lt=Nara period|1=Nara period}}, makkō urushi was commonly used, while kokusō urushi became more prevalent in the Heian period. Once the statue's form is complete, the back or an inconspicuous area is cut open to remove the clay core, and the interior is reinforced with wooden frames to prevent deformation. Examples of this technique include statues at {{ill|Tōdai-ji|en|Tōdai-ji|qid=Q460367|lt=Tōdai-ji|1=Tōdai-ji}}'s Hokkedō, {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|qid=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}}, and {{ill|Tōshōdai-ji|en|Tōshōdai-ji|qid=Q973175|lt=Tōshōdai-ji|1=Tōshōdai-ji}}. Despite its historical significance, the labor-intensive process and high lacquer cost led to the technique's decline after the Heian period. The {{ill|Four Heavenly Kings|lt=statues of the Four Heavenly Kings|ja|四天王像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four Heavenly Kings|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} at Nara's {{ill|Taima-dera|en|Taima-dera|qid=Q478979|lt=Taimadera|1=Taima-dera}} are among the oldest examples of hollow dry lacquer in Japan. === Wood-core Dry Lacquer === In this technique, a statue's basic form is carved in wood, over which hemp cloth is a11lied and finished with makkō urushi or kokusō urushi. Unlike hollow dry lacquer statues, wood-core sculptures retain their wooden cores, and the cloth layers are relatively thin. Early Heian Buddhist statues often combined wood carving with wood-core dry lacquer for features like facial expressions and ornaments, making it difficult to distinguish between "wooden" and "wood-core dry lacquer" statues in some cases. Wood-core dry lacquer statues are less prone to shrinkage than hollow dry lacquer statues, making them suitable for creating fuller, more rounded forms.<ref name="chokoku">Kunō, Ken (ed.), ''Sculpture'' <Japanese History Mini-Encyclopedia>, Kondō Publishing, 1985, 11. 126–129.</ref> These statues can be categorized by their wooden core structure:<ref name="chokoku"/> ;Wooden-skeleton Wood-core Dry Lacquer Statues :Replace the hemp layers in hollow dry lacquer with thinly carved wood. ;Joined-wood Wood-core Dry Lacquer Statues :Thicker wood layers provide strength, with the inner wood removed. ;Single-block Wood-core Dry Lacquer Statues :The core is carved from a single block of wood, often hollowed to reduce weight and prevent cracking. ;Assembled Wood-core Dry Lacquer Statues :A simplified joined-wood structure without the precision of later {{ill|yosegi-zukuri|ja|寄木造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/yosegi-zukuri|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏像|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 仏像|1=Buddha in art|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1000809}} techniques. == Notable Examples == * {{ill|Kōfuku-ji|en|Kōfuku-ji|qid=Q1070863|lt=Kōfuku-ji|1=Kōfuku-ji}} Eight Deva Statues (including the famous Ashura statue) * Kōfuku-ji Ten Great Disciples Statues * {{ill|Fujii-dera|en|Fujii-dera|qid=Q6406788|lt=Fujii-dera|1=Fujii-dera}} (Osaka) Thousand-Armed Kannon Seated Statue === Wood-core Dry Lacquer Buddhist Statues === * Tōshōdai-ji Golden Hall Thousand-Armed Kannon Standing Statue, Yakushi Nyorai Standing Statue * {{ill|Shōrin-ji|ja|聖林寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōrin-ji|qq=}} (Nara) Eleven-faced Kannon Standing Statue * {{ill|Gwaneumsa|en|Gwaneumsa|qid=Q840756|lt=Kannon-ji|1=Gwaneumsa}} (Kyoto) Eleven-faced Kannon Standing Statue * Kōfuku-ji Northern Round Hall Four Heavenly Kings Standing Statues === Wooden Statues with Wood-core Dry Lacquer Features === * {{ill|Tō-ji|en|Tō-ji|qid=Q1046403|lt=Tō-ji|1=Tō-ji}} Lecture Hall Five Great Bodhisattva Statues * {{ill|Kanshin-ji|en|Kanshin-ji|qid=Q1088470|lt=Kanshin-ji|1=Kanshin-ji}} (Osaka) Nyoirin Kannon Seated Statue * {{ill|Jingo-ji|en|Jingo-ji|qid=Q1075034|lt=Jingo-ji|1=Jingo-ji}} Five Great Void Treasury Bodhisattva Seated Statues == Notes == {{Reflist|qq=}}
== See Also == * {{ill|Buddha in art|en|Buddha in art|qid=Q1000809|lt=Buddhist statues|1=Buddha in art}} * {{ill|Lacquerware|ja|漆工|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lacquerware|1=lacquering|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10396786}} * {{ill|Fibre-reinforced plastic|en|Fibre-reinforced plastic|qid=Q263125|lt=Fiber-reinforced plastic|1=Fibre-reinforced plastic}} * {{ill|Composite material|en|Composite material|qid=Q181790|lt=Composite materials|1=Composite material}} * {{ill|Mummy#China|lt=Lacquer-covered mummy statues|ja|ミイラ#中国|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mummy#China|1=Mummy|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q43616}} – Mummies of high-Ranking monks covered in dry lacquer.
Kabe Island
{{translated page|ja|乾漆造|version=104431390|comment=Imported full ja history}}
